So the code would be:

vector<int> maxScoreIndices(vector<int>& nums) {

    int total_ones = accumulate(nums.begin(), nums.end(), 0);

    int left_zeros = 0;

    int right_ones = total_ones;

    int max_score = -1;

    vector<int> result;

    // process split i=0

    int current_score = left_zeros + right_ones;

    process(current_score, 0, max_score, result);

    // process splits i=1 to i=n

    for (int i = 0; i < nums.size(); ++i) {

        if (nums[i] == 0) {

            left_zeros += 1;

        } else {

            right_ones -= 1;

        }

        current_score = left_zeros + right_ones;

        process(current_score, i+1, max_score, result);

    }

    return result;

}

Where process is a helper function that checks the current_score and updates max_score and result accordingly.

So in C++:

class Solution {

public:

    vector<int> maxScoreIndices(vector<int>& nums) {

        int total_ones = accumulate(nums.begin(), nums.end(), 0);

        int left_zeros = 0;

        int right_ones = total_ones;

        int max_score = -1;

        vector<int> result;

        // process split i=0

        int current_score = left_zeros + right_ones;

        if (current_score > max_score) {

            max_score = current_score;

            result = {0};

        } else if (current_score == max_score) {

            result.push_back(0);

        }

        // process splits i=1 to n

        for (int i = 0; i < nums.size(); ++i) {

            if (nums[i] == 0) {

                ++left_zeros;

            } else {

                --right_ones;

            }

            current_score = left_zeros + right_ones;

            if (current_score > max_score) {

                max_score = current_score;

                result = {i+1};

            } else if (current_score == max_score) {

                result.push_back(i+1);

            }

        }

        return result;

    }

};

Now, considering the constraints. The code runs in O(n) time. Because we compute total_ones in O(n), then the loop is O(n). The space is O(1) except for the output. Which is acceptable.

Now, implement this in C++. The starter code is given.

Now, in C++, accumulate is in <numeric>. So need to include that. But the code may assume that.

So the code:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> maxScoreIndices(vector<int>& nums) {
        int total_ones = accumulate(nums.begin(), nums.end(), 0);
        int left_zeros = 0;
        int right_ones = total_ones;
        int max_score = -1;
        vector<int> result;
        // Process split 0
        int current_score = left_zeros + right_ones;
        if (current_score > max_score) {
            max_score = current_score;
            result = {0};
        } else if (current_score == max_score) {
            result.push_back(0);
        }
        // Process splits 1 to n
        for (int i = 0; i < nums.size(); ++i) {
            if (nums[i] == 0) {
                ++left_zeros;
            } else {
                --right_ones;
            }
            int current_score = left_zeros + right_ones;
            if (current_score > max_score) {
                max_score = current_score;
                result = {i+1};
            } else if (current_score == max_score) {
                result.push_back(i+1);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

This should work. Time complexity O(n), space O(1) except for the output. The efficiency is HIGH. Energy implications are LOW since it's O(n) and no extra space except output.

The key idea is to track the left zeros and right ones in a single pass after initial setup. The main strength is linear time, weakness could be handling edge cases, but seems covered.
</think>

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> maxScoreIndices(vector<int>& nums) {
        int total_ones = accumulate(nums.begin(), nums.end(), 0);
        int left_zeros = 0;
        int right_ones = total_ones;
        int max_score = -1;
        vector<int> result;
        // Process split i=0
        int current_score = left_zeros + right_ones;
        if (current_score > max_score) {
            max_score = current_score;
            result = {0};
        } else if (current_score == max_score) {
            result.push_back(0);
        }
        // Process splits i=1 to n
        for (int i = 0; i < nums.size(); ++i) {
            if (nums[i] == 0) {
                ++left_zeros;
            } else {
                --right_ones;
            }
            current_score = left_zeros + right_ones;
            if (current_score > max_score) {
                max_score = current_score;
                result = {i+1};
            } else if (current_score == max_score) {
                result.push_back(i+1);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1) (excluding output)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution efficiently tracks the number of 0s in the left partition and 1s in the right partition using a single pass. It initializes with the total 1s count and updates left/right counts incrementally as it iterates through the array. This linear approach ensures optimal time and space efficiency. Key strength is O(n) performance; minor weakness is output vector growth, but this is unavoidable.
